questions.scenario: A security scanner reads untrusted binary manifests on short-lived Linux workers. The current Python version needs 900 ms to start, can exceed 700 MB on malformed input, and requires a runtime on every worker. The team can accept a longer compile step, but the deployed tool must start quickly and enforce strict memory handling. Which decision best matches the evidence?

options.text: Keep Python solely because a managed runtime removes the need to test malformed input and memory growth
Prototype the parser in Rust because native startup, explicit data ownership, and strong type checks address the stated constraints
Choose Rust because compilation guarantees the parser has no logic bugs or runtime failures
Choose either language based only on benchmark throughput because deployment and failure behavior are equivalent

questions.explanation: Rust is a credible fit because the workload values native execution, predictable resource use, and compiler-enforced data rules. That does not remove testing or guarantee correct parsing, so the proposal still needs benchmarks and malformed-input tests. Keeping Python may remain reasonable if its operational costs are acceptable, but it does not directly address the stated runtime and memory constraints. [The Rust Programming Language](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/) introduces Rust's performance and reliability goals.

questions.scenario:
A security team refactors a configuration loader so validation happens before the same text is recorded in an audit message. The build now fails with:

```text
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `config`
  --> src/main.rs:9:28
5 | validate(config);
  |          ------ value moved here
9 | println!("loaded {}", config);
  |                            ^^^^^^ value borrowed here after move
```

Validation only reads the text, and the audit requirement means the caller must retain it. Which change expresses that contract best?

options.text: Change `validate` to accept `&str` and pass a borrow of `config`
Clone `config` at every call site so ownership questions remain hidden from the function signature
Make `config` mutable because mutation restores a value after it has moved
Suppress E0382 because the later print proves the original allocation still exists

questions.explanation: A shared borrow lets `validate` read the text without taking ownership, so the caller can use `config` afterward. Cloning could work but adds allocation and obscures the read-only contract. Mutability does not undo a move, and the diagnostic identifies a real ownership violation rather than a warning to suppress. [Rust references and borrowing](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch04-02-references-and-borrowing.html) explains this data flow.

questions.scenario: A product group is choosing implementation languages. Project Atlas is a command-line parser for large untrusted files with tight startup and memory budgets. Project Beacon is a two-page internal form backed by an existing TypeScript platform, and its delivery deadline is two weeks. The team has Rust experience but no mandate to standardize on one language. Which recommendation is most defensible?

options.text: Reject Rust for Atlas because memory-safe code cannot parse untrusted binary formats efficiently
Use Rust for both because native compilation is the dominant requirement for every production application
Use Rust for Atlas, then evaluate Beacon against team speed and platform integration instead of assuming the same answer
Use TypeScript for Atlas because an existing web platform proves it meets command-line startup and memory constraints

questions.explanation: Atlas aligns with Rust's strengths: native deployment, performance, explicit memory behavior, and safe parsing. Beacon has different constraints, where the existing platform and short deadline may outweigh Rust's benefits. A sound language decision follows workload and team evidence, not a universal rule. [Rust's production users](https://www.rust-lang.org/production) provides examples of where those tradeoffs are valuable.

questions.scenario: A configuration service has two operations. Reading `/etc/app.toml` can fail with an I/O or parsing error. Looking up an optional `owner` field can legitimately return no value. A reviewer proposes returning an empty string from both operations. Which API design preserves the operational difference?

options.text: Use empty strings for both because callers can infer every failure cause from string length
Return `Option<Config>` for loading and discard whether the file was missing, unreadable, or malformed
Return `Result<&str, ConfigError>` for the owner and treat normal absence as a service failure
Return `Result<Config, ConfigError>` for loading and `Option<&str>` for the optional owner

questions.explanation: `Result` preserves recoverable failure details from loading, while `Option` represents an expected present-or-absent field. Collapsing load failures into `None` loses useful diagnosis, and treating ordinary absence as an error overstates the condition. Empty-string sentinels make every caller rediscover an undocumented convention. [Result](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/) and [Option](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/option/) document these contracts.

questions.scenario: During onboarding, a developer spends an hour changing code around a compiler error without reading it. The diagnostic names the moved binding, the move site, the later use, and suggests borrowing. The team wants a learning practice that also improves production review quality. What should the mentor recommend?

options.text: Reduce the case to a small program, trace the diagnostic locations, apply one ownership change, then verify behavior with a test
Add clones until compilation succeeds, then avoid revisiting the ownership contract during review
Move directly to unsafe pointers so compiler diagnostics no longer constrain the design
Memorize the error number because the same number has one universal fix in every program

questions.explanation: The diagnostic is structured evidence about data flow. A small reproduction makes that evidence easier to follow, and a test connects compilation with intended behavior. Cloning can be deliberate, but using it blindly hides design questions. Unsafe code and memorized error numbers do not replace understanding the binding, move, and later use. [Rust compiler error E0382](https://doc.rust-lang.org/error_codes/E0382.html) shows how move diagnostics guide corrections.

questions.scenario: A team is approving a four-week Rust pilot for a native log scrubber. The exit criteria are a reproducible build, safe handling of borrowed input, explicit error paths, and evidence that the tool fits its workload. Which activities directly support those criteria? Select all that apply.

options.text: Benchmark representative inputs and record startup, memory, and throughput against the current tool
Treat successful compilation as proof that malformed production inputs cannot trigger incorrect behavior
Practice compiler diagnostics on small ownership and borrowing examples before expanding the parser
Adopt nightly-only features immediately so the pilot cannot be evaluated on the stable toolchain described in the article
Model fallible parsing with `Result` and expected absence with `Option`, then test both branches

questions.explanation: Measurements test project fit, explicit return types expose failure contracts, and focused compiler exercises build the data-flow skills the implementation needs. Nightly may be justified by a specific feature, but making it a default adds avoidable compatibility risk. Compilation catches important classes of mistakes, but malformed-input tests are still required. [The Rust Programming Language](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/) follows this progression from tooling and ownership to error handling and projects.
